agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Decompose the courier platform monolith into 5 bounded contexts mapped to 5 services, prioritizing deployment independence between the highest-change-velocity components (payments, location tracking). The decomposition follows domain boundaries rather than technical layers, uses asynchronous domain events for cross-service communication where possible, and preserves synchronous calls only where transactional consistency is non-negotiable. For an 8-person team, 5 services is the upper bound — more would exceed operational capacity given moderate maturity.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Order lifecycle: placement, state machine (placed → assigned → picked-up → delivered → completed/cancelled), order queries. Owns the order aggregate and orchestrates the fulfillment workflow by emitting events and reacting to events from other services.","boundaries":"Owns orders table. Does NOT own courier assignments directly — requests assignment via event/command to Courier Service. Does NOT process payments — delegates to Payment Service. Exposes REST API for customer-facing order operations and an internal gRPC API for back-office."},{"name":"Courier Service","responsibility":"Courier profiles, availability management, assignment algorithm, route optimization, and ingestion of real-time location updates. This is the write-heavy service (5K writes/sec location telemetry). Exposes a location streaming endpoint (WebSocket or gRPC streaming) for consumers.","boundaries":"Owns courier profiles, courier availability, assignment records, location history, and route data. Location writes go to a time-series or append-optimized store (TimescaleDB or Redis Streams with periodic flush). Does NOT own order state — receives assignment requests via events from Order Service and publishes assignment-completed/courier-location-updated events."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Payment intent creation, charge execution, refund processing, payment state machine, ledger entries. Integrates with external payment gateway (Stripe, Adyen, etc.). Enforces strict transactional consistency within its boundary.","boundaries":"Owns payment records, refund records, ledger. Isolated database with ACID guarantees — no shared DB with other services. Communicates via synchronous request-reply for charge/refund operations (called by Order Service) and emits payment-succeeded/payment-failed events asynchronously."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Fan-out of notifications across channels (email, SMS, push). Template management, delivery tracking, retry logic, rate limiting. Consumes domain events and translates them into user-facing notifications.","boundaries":"Owns notification templates, delivery logs, user channel preferences. Purely reactive — never called synchronously. Subscribes to events from Order, Courier, and Payment services. Integrates with external providers (SendGrid, Twilio, Firebase)."},{"name":"Customer Service","responsibility":"Customer account CRUD, authentication, profile management, address book, preferences. Identity provider integration if applicable.","boundaries":"Owns customer profiles, auth credentials, addresses. Other services reference customers by ID only — they do not query or mutate customer data directly. Exposes a lightweight read API for other services to resolve customer details (name, address for delivery)."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Order Service","to":"Courier Service","contract":"AssignCourier command (async via message broker): {orderId, pickupLocation, dropoffLocation, requiredBy}. Courier Service responds with CourierAssigned event {orderId, courierId, estimatedArrival}.","coupling_type":"asynchronous - event/command via broker"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"Synchronous gRPC: ChargePayment(orderId, amount, paymentMethodToken) → {paymentId, status}. Also RefundPayment(paymentId, amount) → {refundId, status}. Synchronous because order placement must confirm payment before proceeding.","coupling_type":"synchronous - request/reply"},{"from":"Courier Service","to":"Order Service","contract":"CourierAssigned, CourierLocationUpdated, DeliveryCompleted events published to broker. Order Service subscribes to update order state machine.","coupling_type":"asynchronous - domain events"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Order Service","contract":"PaymentSucceeded, PaymentFailed, RefundCompleted events. Order Service uses these to confirm or roll back order state.","coupling_type":"asynchronous - domain events"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"OrderPlaced, OrderStatusChanged events consumed by Notification Service to trigger customer notifications. No direct API calls.","coupling_type":"asynchronous - domain events"},{"from":"Courier Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"CourierAssigned, CourierNearby, DeliveryCompleted events trigger push notifications and SMS to customers.","coupling_type":"asynchronous - domain events"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Customer Service","contract":"Synchronous REST/gRPC: GetCustomer(customerId) → {name, defaultAddress, phone}. Read-only, cached aggressively (customer data changes infrequently).","coupling_type":"synchronous - read-only query, cacheable"},{"from":"API Gateway","to":"All Services","contract":"Public REST API routed to appropriate service. Gateway handles auth token validation (JWT issued by Customer Service), rate limiting, and request routing.","coupling_type":"synchronous - HTTP routing"}],"data_flow":"1. Customer places order via API Gateway → Order Service creates order (PENDING_PAYMENT). 2. Order Service calls Payment Service synchronously to charge → on success, order moves to CONFIRMED and emits OrderConfirmed event. 3. Courier Service consumes OrderConfirmed, runs assignment algorithm, publishes CourierAssigned. 4. Order Service consumes CourierAssigned, updates order state to ASSIGNED. 5. Courier app sends GPS pings every 10s → Courier Service ingests via dedicated streaming endpoint (WebSocket/gRPC stream), writes to time-series store, publishes CourierLocationUpdated at reduced frequency (every 30s) for downstream consumers. 6. Notification Service consumes all relevant events and dispatches email/SMS/push as configured. 7. On delivery completion, Courier app signals Courier Service → DeliveryCompleted event → Order Service finalizes order → Notification Service sends confirmation.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Strangler Fig Migration","rationale":"Do not rewrite all 5 services at once. Extract one service at a time from the monolith, starting with the service that provides the most deployment independence value (Payment Service or Courier/Location Service). Route traffic at the API Gateway level, gradually shifting endpoints from monolith to new service."},{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture (Choreography)","rationale":"Services communicate via domain events through a message broker. Reduces temporal coupling — services don't need to be available simultaneously. Fits the team's goal of independent deployability. Choreography over orchestration because the workflow is relatively linear and the team is new to distributed systems."},{"pattern":"CQRS (Courier Service only)","rationale":"Location tracking has a massive write volume (5K/sec) but read patterns differ (latest location vs. history vs. ETA calculation). Separate the write model (append-only location events) from read models (materialized current-location view, route history). Not applied globally — only where write/read asymmetry justifies it."},{"pattern":"API Gateway","rationale":"Single entry point for external clients. Handles cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, TLS termination) so individual services don't duplicate this logic. Also serves as the routing layer for Strangler Fig migration."},{"pattern":"Database-per-Service","rationale":"Each service owns its data store. Eliminates shared-database coupling, which is the #1 reason monolith-to-microservice migrations fail. Enables independent schema evolution and technology choices per service (e.g., TimescaleDB for location, PostgreSQL for payments)."},{"pattern":"Transactional Outbox","rationale":"For Payment Service and Order Service, use the outbox pattern to ensure domain events are published reliably. Write the event to an outbox table in the same transaction as the state change, then a relay process publishes to the broker. Prevents lost events and dual-write inconsistencies."},{"pattern":"Saga (Implicit Choreography-Based)","rationale":"Order placement spans Order + Payment services. Instead of distributed transactions, the order state machine reacts to payment events. On PaymentFailed, the order is cancelled. This is a simple choreography-based saga — no orchestrator needed at this scale."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: orderId, contains line items, delivery address, status, timestamps)","OrderStatusHistory (value object list tracking state transitions)"]},{"name":"Courier Operations","classification":"core","aggregates":["Courier (root: courierId, profile, availability status, current assignment)","CourierLocation (entity: courierId + timestamp + coordinates, stored as time-series)","Assignment (root: assignmentId, links orderId ↔ courierId, status, route)"]},{"name":"Payments","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Payment (root: paymentId, orderId, amount, status, gateway reference)","Refund (root: refundId, paymentId, amount, status, reason)"]},{"name":"Notifications","classification":"generic","aggregates":["NotificationRequest (root: notificationId, channel, recipient, template, status, attempts)"]},{"name":"Customer Identity","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Customer (root: customerId, profile, auth credentials, addresses, preferences)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Order Management publishes events; Notifications conforms to the event schema. Order team sets the pace."},{"upstream":"Courier Operations","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Courier Operations publishes assignment and delivery events that Order Management consumes to advance its state machine."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Courier Operations","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Order Management publishes OrderConfirmed to trigger courier assignment."},{"upstream":"Payments","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Conformist. Order Management conforms to Payment's charge/refund API contract. Payment is upstream because it dictates the payment flow and error semantics."},{"upstream":"Customer Identity","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Open Host Service. Customer Service exposes a stable read API. Order Service queries it but does not influence its model."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"5 services (not fewer, not more)","gains":"Maps cleanly to domain boundaries. Manageable for 8 engineers (roughly 1.5-2 engineers per service). Each service is independently deployable. Clear ownership.","sacrifices":"Could have kept Order + Customer together to reduce network calls. 5 services still creates meaningful operational overhead (5 deployment pipelines, 5 sets of health checks, distributed tracing needed).","alternatives_considered":["3 services (Orders+Customers, Couriers+Location, Payments+Notifications) — fewer operational units but couples unrelated change velocities","7+ services (separate Location Tracking from Courier Management, separate Refunds from Payments) — cleaner SRP but exceeds team's operational capacity"]},{"decision":"Synchronous call for payment charging","gains":"Order placement gets immediate confirmation/rejection. Simpler error handling — no need to show 'pending' state to customer. Matches user expectation of instant checkout.","sacrifices":"Temporal coupling: Order Service depends on Payment Service availability. If Payment Service is down, orders cannot be placed.","alternatives_considered":["Fully async payment — emit PaymentRequested event, show 'processing' to customer, confirm later. Better decoupling but worse UX and more complex state management."]},{"decision":"Choreography over orchestration for the order saga","gains":"No central orchestrator to maintain. Each service owns its own reaction logic. Simpler to start with for a team new to distributed systems.","sacrifices":"Harder to visualize the end-to-end flow. As workflows grow more complex (e.g., multi-stop orders, scheduled deliveries), choreography may become tangled. May need to introduce orchestration later.","alternatives_considered":["Saga orchestrator in Order Service — explicit workflow definition, easier to reason about, but creates a God service that knows about all other services."]},{"decision":"CQRS only in Courier Service, not globally","gains":"Addresses the actual bottleneck (5K writes/sec) without imposing complexity everywhere. Other services use simple CRUD patterns.","sacrifices":"Inconsistency in architectural style across services — developers context-switching between patterns.","alternatives_considered":["Global CQRS — uniform pattern but unjustified complexity for Order, Customer, Notification services","No CQRS anywhere — simpler but 5K writes/sec to a normalized relational model will hit limits"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Notification channels","evolution_path":"New channels (WhatsApp, in-app) are added by implementing a new channel adapter in Notification Service. No other service changes. Template system allows non-engineer content updates."},{"area":"Assignment algorithm","evolution_path":"The assignment logic is encapsulated in Courier Service. Can evolve from simple nearest-courier to ML-based optimization without touching Order Service."},{"area":"Payment gateway","evolution_path":"Payment Service abstracts the gateway behind an internal interface. Switching from Stripe to Adyen (or supporting both) is contained within Payment Service."},{"area":"Location data store","evolution_path":"Courier Service can migrate from PostgreSQL to TimescaleDB to InfluxDB as write volume grows. No other service is affected because location data is accessed only through Courier Service APIs/events."},{"area":"Read models for tracking","evolution_path":"CQRS in Courier Service allows adding new read models (e.g., ETA prediction, heatmaps, analytics) without impacting the write path."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Order-Payment synchronous coupling","reason":"Order placement depends on Payment Service being available. This is an intentional rigidity — the business requires payment confirmation before order acceptance. Circuit breaker and retry patterns mitigate but don't eliminate this coupling."},{"area":"Event schema contracts","reason":"Once services depend on event schemas (OrderConfirmed, CourierAssigned, etc.), changing these schemas requires coordinated evolution. Use schema versioning (e.g., CloudEvents with explicit version field) from day one."},{"area":"Customer ID as cross-cutting reference","reason":"All services reference customers by customerId. The format and semantics of this ID are rigid — changing it would require coordinated migration across all services."},{"area":"Message broker dependency","reason":"The broker (Kafka, RabbitMQ, or similar) becomes critical infrastructure. All async communication depends on it. Must be treated as a first-class operational concern with HA configuration."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Operational overhead exceeds team capacity. Moving from 1 deployable to 5 with only 8 engineers and moderate operational maturity. Need CI/CD pipelines, distributed tracing, centralized logging, health monitoring per service.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Invest in platform tooling BEFORE extracting services. Standardize deployment (containerized, same CI template). Use managed services where possible (managed Kafka, managed DB). Extract services one at a time via Strangler Fig — never run more services than the team can operate."},{"risk":"Data consistency across services. Without distributed transactions, there's a window where Order says CONFIRMED but Payment hasn't processed, or courier is assigned but order doesn't reflect it yet.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Transactional outbox pattern ensures events are published reliably. Idempotent event handlers prevent duplicate processing. Compensating actions (cancel order on PaymentFailed) handle failures. Accept eventual consistency as a feature, not a bug — design UIs to show transitional states gracefully."},{"risk":"Location tracking write throughput. 5K writes/sec is significant. If Courier Service uses a standard PostgreSQL setup, it may struggle under peak load.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"CQRS with append-only writes. Use a time-series optimized store (TimescaleDB, or buffer in Redis Streams and batch-flush to persistent storage). Horizontal scaling of the write endpoint is straightforward since location updates are partitioned by courierId."},{"risk":"Strangler Fig migration stalls. The team extracts 2 services then loses momentum, leaving a hybrid monolith + microservices architecture that's worse than either pure approach.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Define a clear extraction sequence with deadlines. Extraction order: 1) Notification Service (lowest risk, purely reactive), 2) Customer Service (simple CRUD, low coupling), 3) Payment Service (high value — strict consistency boundary), 4) Courier Service (most complex, highest write volume), 5) Order Service (last, since it's the orchestrating core). Each extraction is a milestone with a retrospective."},{"risk":"Team lacks distributed systems debugging skills. Monolith developers may struggle with network partitions, message ordering, and distributed tracing.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Training before migration begins. Adopt OpenTelemetry for distributed tracing from the first extracted service. Invest in structured logging with correlation IDs. Run chaos engineering exercises after each extraction."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Strangler Fig migration, extracting one service at a time over 6-9 months. Start with Notification Service (lowest risk, event-driven by nature, minimal data coupling). Then Customer Service (simple, well-defined boundary). Then Payment Service (high business value for isolation). Then Courier Service (most complex). Order Service is extracted last since it's the natural orchestrator and will be the last to fully decouple. Before extracting ANY service, establish: (1) API Gateway, (2) message broker, (3) CI/CD pipeline template, (4) distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry), (5) centralized logging.","files_to_create_or_modify":["docs/adr/001-microservice-decomposition.md — Architecture Decision Record capturing this decomposition rationale","docs/adr/002-event-driven-communication.md — ADR for choosing choreography + events over orchestration","docs/adr/003-cqrs-courier-location.md — ADR for CQRS in Courier Service only","docs/context-map.md — Visual context map showing service relationships","infra/docker-compose.yml — Local development environment with all 5 services + broker + gateway","services/notification-service/ — First service to extract (scaffold)","services/shared/events/ — Shared event schema definitions (CloudEvents format, versioned)","services/shared/proto/ — gRPC proto definitions for synchronous interfaces"],"patterns_to_follow":"Each service follows hexagonal architecture internally: domain core (entities, value objects, domain services) → application layer (use cases, event handlers) → infrastructure (DB adapters, message broker adapters, HTTP/gRPC controllers). This keeps each service internally clean and testable while allowing different infrastructure choices per service.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Shared database — NEVER let two services read/write the same database. This is the #1 migration failure mode.","Distributed monolith — if every request requires synchronous calls to 3+ services, you've built a distributed monolith. Minimize synchronous dependencies.","Extracting too many services at once — extract one, stabilize, retrospect, then extract the next.","Skipping the outbox pattern — dual writes (update DB + publish event) WILL lose events under failure. Use transactional outbox from day one.","Over-engineering Notification Service — it's a generic subdomain. Keep it simple. Don't gold-plate it.","Premature performance optimization — 5K writes/sec is achievable with a well-configured PostgreSQL + TimescaleDB. Don't jump to Cassandra or custom solutions until you've proven PostgreSQL can't handle it."]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Service boundary decomposition aligned to domain boundaries","5 services as the right count for an 8-person team","CQRS for Courier Service location tracking","Strangler Fig as migration strategy","Event-driven communication between services"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact write throughput characteristics — 5K/sec is manageable but need to validate with actual payload sizes and indexing requirements","Team's readiness for operational complexity — moderate maturity is vague, actual capability may be higher or lower","Whether choreography will remain sufficient as the business logic grows — may need orchestration within 12-18 months"],"assumptions":["The 8 engineers are full-stack or backend-focused and can own services end-to-end","The team has or will acquire Kubernetes/container orchestration capability","A managed message broker (Kafka, RabbitMQ, or cloud-native equivalent) is available or budgeted","The monolith's existing data model has reasonably clear seams along the identified domain boundaries — if the database is heavily cross-joined, extraction will be significantly harder"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Decompose the courier platform monolith into 5 bounded contexts mapped to 5 services, prioritizing deployment independence between the highest-change-velocity components (payments, location tracking). The decomposition follows domain boundaries rather than technical layers, uses asynchronous domain events for cross-service communication where possible, and preserves synchronous calls only where transactional consistency is non-negotiable. For an 8-person team, 5 services is
